title: Verilog vs SystemVerilog vs VHDL
description: VHDL is strongly typed and verbose; Verilog is terse and weakly typed; SystemVerilog adds verification features (classes, assertions, interfaces).
body: Use when choosing or porting between HDLs. VHDL catches width/type mismatches at elaboration (strong typing) but is verbose; Verilog is concise but silently truncates/extends widths; SystemVerilog extends Verilog with always_ff/always_comb, interfaces, packed/unpacked types, classes, and SVA for verification. Do not assume Verilog will catch a width mismatch VHDL would. Actions: match constructs to each language's rules and use SystemVerilog's verification layer for testbenches. Validation signals include elaboration warnings on width mismatch. Risk boundary: a silent Verilog width mismatch is a real functional bug; enable and read width warnings.
